York State appeals court dismissed lawsuits against Take-Two Interactive which Lindsay Lohan Wives star Karen Gravano claimed that Grand Theft Auto violated their privacy misappropriating their images.
2014, Lohan sued makers wildly popular open-world action-adventure arguing that company used likeness create game's character Lacey Jonas without permission.
Reality star Gravano also filed similar suit stating that elements likeness life story were stolen create character Andrea Bottino story arc.
Since both cases shared multiple similarities, panel five judges decided combine complaints.
"Even accept plaintiffs' contentions that video game depictions close enough considered representations respective plaintiffs, plaintiffs' claims should dismissed because this video game does fall under statutory definitions 'advertising' 'trade,'" court's ruling reads.
"This video game's unique story, characters, dialogue, environment, combined with player's ability choose proceed game, render work fiction satire."
Lohan's suit claimed that character Jonas "unequivocal" reference Mean Girls actress, adding that incorporated image, likeness, clothing clothing line products form "hats, hair style, sunglasses jean shorts".
They also cited game's inclusion Chateau Marmont Hotel West Hollywood where actress once lived evidence image rights violation.
Parent Trap actress' 67-page complaint focused images that alleged were Lohan look-alikes featuring "red bikini girl" striking "peace sign" pose that shown cover game's intro other woman shown being frisked that appears cover game disc.
players help fictional Jonas character escape from paparazzi downtown Vinewood drive home.

look good?"

They said Lohan merely suing "publicity purposes".
Gravano, daughter jailed mobster Salvatore "Sammy Bull" Gravano, also similar claims regarding Bottino.
argued that Bottino used same phrases does whose story included several similarities real-life story.

However, appellate panel Manhattan disagreed decided dismiss both suits saying even video game images were "close enough considered representations", video games considered protected works fiction satire.
added that "the images Lohan herself, merely avatar game that Lohan claims depiction her".

Lohan's attorney, Robert Pritchard, immediate comment, Reuters reports.
